---
title: ""
page-layout: full
toc: false
title-block-banner: false
---

::: {.about-hero}
::: {.site-container}

<blockquote class="about-scripture">
“My grace is sufficient for you, for My strength is made perfect in weakness.”
<footer><em>2 Corinthians 12:9</em></footer>
</blockquote>

:::
:::


::: {.about-section}
::: {.site-container}

::: {.about-layout}

::: {.about-main}

## Hi! This is me...

I am a Ph.D. student in the [Lew Klein College of Media and Communication’s](https://www.temple.edu/academics/degree-programs/media-communication-phd-co-mdcm-phd),
where I study the relationships among artificial intelligence, communication,
institutions, and public life.

My work brings together Human–Machine Communication, computational social
science, AI governance, and critical technology studies. I use computational
and qualitative methods to investigate how AI systems are imagined, developed,
governed, represented, and experienced across different cultural and
institutional settings.

At the center of my research is a concern with power. I am interested in who
defines the language of responsible AI, whose knowledge becomes embedded in
technological systems, how institutions distribute responsibility when those
systems cause harm, and how communities that have historically been peripheral
to technological development are positioned within global AI futures.

Much of my work pays particular attention to Africa and the Global South. I do
not approach these regions merely as sites where technologies are adopted or
where familiar theories are applied. I study them as places where people,
institutions, and communities actively negotiate technological change, produce
knowledge, challenge dominant assumptions, and imagine alternative digital
futures.


## From Federal Polytechnic Oko to Graduate Study

My academic journey began at
[Federal Polytechnic Oko](https://federalpolyoko.edu.ng/), where I earned a
Higher National Diploma in Mass Communication. My training introduced me to
journalism, broadcasting, public communication, media production, and the processes through which societies interpret change, and the professional
routines that determine what becomes visible.

During my master's degree in Communication Studies at 
[Northern Illinois University](https://www.niu.edu/comm/), I became increasingly interested in the arrival of artificial
intelligence in newsrooms. I wanted to understand AI not only as a collection
of tools, but also as a communicative presence that journalists were learning
to interpret, trust, question, and incorporate into their work.

My master's research was advised by
[Dr. Andrea L. Guzman](https://www.niu.edu/comm/contact-us/directory/guzman-andrea.shtml),
whose scholarship helped establish Human–Machine Communication as an important
area of communication research.

My thesis, *Journalists’ Perception of Artificial Intelligence: The Case of
Nigerian Newsrooms*, drew on in-depth interviews with reporters, editors,
producers, and fact-checkers. The study examined how Nigerian journalists made
sense of AI within their everyday professional practices.

The journalists I interviewed described AI as a source of speed, efficiency,
and creative assistance, but they also raised concerns about verification,
cultural accuracy, professional judgment, newsroom policy, and the limitations
of systems developed without adequate knowledge of African contexts. Their
experiences showed that the adoption of AI is never simply technical. It is
shaped by institutional resources, professional norms, cultural expectations,
and unequal technological infrastructures.

That project provided the foundation for my continuing work on AI, journalism,
trust, and institutional change. It also strengthened my commitment to
conducting research that places African experiences within broader theoretical
and policy conversations about emerging technologies.


## Doctoral Research at Temple

I am now pursuing my Ph.D. under the guidance of
[Dr. Andrew Iliadis](https://andrewiliadis.com/) at Temple University's Lew
Klein College of Media and Communication.

My doctoral work expands beyond the newsroom to examine the broader social,
institutional, and infrastructural life of artificial intelligence. I study how
technology companies, governments, media organizations, consultants, and
international institutions construct ideas about AI safety, responsibility,
risk, development, and technological progress.

My research considers both the discourse surrounding AI and the material
systems that support it. I am especially interested in the relationships among
data infrastructures, institutional authority, corporate governance, platform
power, and global inequality.

Across my doctoral projects, I ask questions such as:

- How do corporations define and allocate responsibility for AI systems?
- How do governance frameworks change as they move across regions?
- How are Africa and Latin America constructed within narratives of AI expansion?
- How do people develop trust in systems that are difficult to understand or evaluate?
- How can computational methods support critical and culturally grounded communication research?


## Current Research

One strand of my work examines how leading AI companies describe responsibility
in their official governance, safety, and evaluation documents. Through
computational analysis, I study how companies distribute responsibility among
models, developers, users, organizational procedures, standards, and
institutions.

A second strand investigates the movement of AI governance frameworks across
national and regional contexts. This work examines the relationship between
European regulatory influence, African data protection laws, national AI
strategies, institutional capacity, and local development priorities.

My project on the Global South as a speculative AI market analyzes how
technology companies, consultancies, policy organizations, and investment
actors construct Africa and Latin America as emerging frontiers for AI
deployment. Rather than treating markets as already existing, the study
examines how discourse actively produces expectations about future consumers,
workers, infrastructures, and opportunities.

I am also conducting computational research on deepfake coverage across African
newspapers. This project studies what journalists are talking about when they
report on deepfakes, including elections, criminality, sexual and gendered
harm, regulation, public awareness, technological innovation, and geopolitical
risk.

Alongside my work on AI, I study digital culture more broadly. My research on
Nigerian podcasting examines how conversational programs create spaces for
parasocial intimacy, pragmatic feminism, digital sisterhood, and the
renegotiation of contemporary Nigerian womanhood.

Although these projects address different media and institutional settings,
they share a common concern with how communication constructs technological
meaning and how those meanings shape power, policy, and public life.


## Methods and Research Practice

I work across computational, qualitative, and mixed-methods traditions. My
computational research includes natural language processing, computational text
analysis, semantic similarity, embeddings, classification, topic modeling,
network analysis, entity analysis, statistical comparison, web-based data
collection, and data visualization.

I primarily use Python and R, alongside tools and libraries such as Jupyter,
pandas, spaCy, scikit-learn, quanteda, ATLAS.ti, Git, and GitHub.

My qualitative training includes in-depth interviewing, thematic analysis,
critical discourse analysis, framing analysis, and comparative media research.
I approach computational methods as tools for answering substantive social
questions rather than as substitutes for theory, interpretation, or contextual
knowledge.

This combination allows me to move between large-scale patterns and close
attention to language, institutions, professional practice, and lived
experience.


## Research Beyond Academia

Before beginning my Ph.D., I developed a professional career across journalism,
communications, education, corporate work, and technology-adjacent
organizational environments.

I worked as a field journalist with the Anambra State Ministry of
Transportation, an experience that gave me direct exposure to public
institutions, reporting, government communication, and the relationship
between policy and everyday life.

I later worked in communications and corporate strategy, eventually progressing
into a senior specialist role at KeHE Distributors. In that environment, I
worked within a large and complex organization where communication, data,
coordination, and operational decisions had to align across teams. The
experience strengthened my understanding of how institutional priorities are
implemented in practice, and how organizational incentives can differ from
formal policies and stated values.

I also worked with the Illinois Tutoring Initiative, a statewide program
supporting K–12 learners. That role connected my interests in education and
access with direct work in a structured learning environment.

These experiences continue to shape my academic questions. My
research on governance and responsibility is informed not only by scholarship,
but also by firsthand familiarity with the routines, constraints, and
decision-making processes of real organizations.


## Where I Am Headed

My long-term goal is to contribute to the development and evaluation of
trustworthy, accountable, and socially grounded artificial intelligence.

I am particularly interested in research roles that connect computational
analysis with questions of AI risk, trust and safety, information integrity,
human–AI interaction, institutional accountability, and responsible
technological development.

I hope to build a career that moves across academia, industry, and public
policy. I want my research to contribute to theory, but I also want it to inform
the design of governance systems, evaluation practices, organizational
standards, and technologies that respond more thoughtfully to the diversity of
the societies in which they are deployed.

Ultimately, my work is driven by the belief that artificial intelligence
is not only a technical system. It is also a communicative, institutional, and
political project. Understanding its consequences requires paying attention to
the people who build it, the organizations that govern it, the stories through
which it is made meaningful, and the communities whose futures are increasingly
shaped by it.

:::


::: {.about-sidebar}

::: {.about-card}


### Academic Trajectory

<div class="trajectory-item">

<span class="trajectory-year">2025–Present</span>

**Ph.D. in Media and Communication**  
<em>[Temple University](https://www.temple.edu/academics/degree-programs/media-communication-phd-co-mdcm-phd)</em>

Presidential Fellow

</div>

<div class="trajectory-item">

<span class="trajectory-year">2022–2024</span>

**M.A. in Communication Studies**  
<em>Northern Illinois University</em>

Concentration in Human–Machine Communication

</div>

<div class="trajectory-item">

<span class="trajectory-year">2010–2016</span>

**Higher National Diploma in Mass Communication**  
<em>Federal Polytechnic Oko, Anambra State, Nigeria</em>

Foundation in journalism, media studies, broadcasting, public relations, and communication research.

</div>

:::

::: {.about-card}

### Scholarly Mentors

<div class="trajectory-item">

**[Andrew Iliadis](https://andrewiliadis.com/)**

Ph.D. Advisor  
<em>Temple University</em>

Artificial intelligence, semantic infrastructures, data science, digital media, and critical infrastructure studies.

</div>

<div class="trajectory-item">

**[Andrea L. Guzman](https://www.niu.edu/comm/contact-us/directory/guzman-andrea.shtml)**

Master's Advisor  
<em>Northern Illinois University</em>

Human–Machine Communication, artificial intelligence, and communication theory.

</div>

<div class="trajectory-item">

**[David J. Gunkel](https://gunkelweb.com/)**

Master's Thesis Committee  
<em>Northern Illinois University</em>

AI ethics, philosophy of technology, robotics, and Human–Machine Communication.

</div>

<div class="trajectory-item">

**[Bill Cassidy](https://olemiss.edu/profiles/bcassidy)**

Master's Thesis Committee  
<em>University of Mississippi (formerly Northern Illinois University)</em>

Journalism, media sociology, influences on media content, and content analysis.

</div>

:::

::: {.about-card}

### Research Appointments

<div class="experience-item">

<span class="experience-period">2026–Present</span>

**Duckworth Scholars Studio Extern**  
<em>Temple University</em>

Conducting interdisciplinary computational media research through the Loretta C. Duckworth Scholars Studio while collaborating on digital scholarship and computational communication projects.

</div>

<div class="experience-item">

<span class="experience-period">2025–Present</span>

**Ph.D. Researcher**  
<em>Temple University</em>

Conducting research in artificial intelligence, Human–Machine Communication, computational social science, and digital media.

</div>

:::

::: {.about-card}

### Professional Experience

<div class="experience-item">

<span class="experience-period">2024–2025</span>

**Senior Communications Specialist**  
<em>KeHE Distributors, Inc.</em>

Led strategic communications and cross-functional organizational initiatives before beginning full-time doctoral study.

</div>
<div class="experience-item">

<span class="experience-period">2022–2024</span>

**Graduate Assistant, Illinois Tutoring Initiative**  
<em>Northern Illinois University</em>

Supported the administration of NIU’s Illinois Tutoring Initiative regional hub, which coordinated high-impact tutoring partnerships with school districts across northern Illinois. 
</div>

<div class="experience-item">

<span class="experience-period">May–July 2023</span>

**Communications Intern**  
<em>US Xpress</em>

Supported corporate communications, internal communication initiatives, and organizational projects while completing my master's degree.

</div>

<div class="experience-item">

<span class="experience-period">2020–2022</span>

**Communications & Public Relations Specialist**  
<em>Finotel Classique Hotel & Reservations</em>

Led communication strategy, public relations, marketing, and brand development initiatives.

</div>

<div class="experience-item">

<span class="experience-period">2018–2020</span>

**Field Journalist**  
<em>Anambra State Ministry of Transportation</em>

Covered transportation, infrastructure, and public policy while producing news stories and public communication materials.

</div>

:::

## Service, Mentorship, and Community

My commitment to education extends beyond my own academic journey. I understand education as more than individual achievement. For me, it is also a means of widening access, sharing knowledge, and helping others imagine possibilities that may initially feel out of reach.

Having experienced how difficult it can be to locate trustworthy information, navigate unfamiliar admissions systems, and gain recognition for an HND, I wanted to make that journey less isolating for others.

I founded [HNDScholarsHub](https://x.com/HNDScholarsHub), a nonprofit and peer-support initiative that helps Higher National Diploma holders identify and pursue graduate education opportunities around the world. The initiative shares scholarship opportunities, application guidance, admissions resources, and practical advice for students navigating the path from polytechnic education to master's and doctoral study.

HNDScholarsHub emerged directly from my own experience. I had learned that HND holders could succeed in highly competitive graduate programs, yet accurate information was often fragmented, and many prospective applicants had already been told that their qualifications made further study impossible. Through the initiative, I have worked to challenge that assumption and build a community where HND graduates can learn from one another, ask questions, access reliable information, and pursue their ambitions with greater confidence.

I also founded *Back to School with Amy*, an education and community initiative dedicated to supporting children and families through the provision of school supplies and other educational resources. The initiative reflects my belief that access to education begins long before the classroom, and that small interventions can create meaningful opportunities for young learners.

These initiatives remain closely connected to my research. Whether I am studying the unequal development of artificial intelligence, helping a student navigate the graduate admissions process, or supporting children returning to school, I am ultimately interested in the systems that shape access to knowledge and opportunity. Across both my scholarship and community work, I ask whose knowledge is recognized, who is given the opportunity to participate, and whose future is imagined as possible.

As I continue developing as a scholar, I hope to mentor emerging researchers, particularly students from African and other historically underrepresented backgrounds who are interested in communication, computational research, and the social implications of artificial intelligence. I believe that scholarship carries a responsibility beyond publication. It should also create pathways, build communities, and expand opportunities for those who follow.

### Leadership and Community

<div class="experience-item">

**Founder and Lead, HNDScholarsHub**

A nonprofit and peer-support initiative helping HND holders find graduate
programs, scholarships, and pathways into master's and doctoral education.

[Visit HNDScholarsHub](https://x.com/HNDScholarsHub)

</div>

<div class="experience-item">

**Founder, Back to School with Amy**

A community education initiative providing school supplies and learning support
to children and families.

</div>

:::
:::

::: {.about-sidebar}

::: {.about-card}

### Research Areas

<div class="about-pill-grid">
<span class="about-pill">Human–Machine Communication</span>
<span class="about-pill">Responsible AI</span>
<span class="about-pill">AI Risk, Trust & Safety</span>
<span class="about-pill">Computational Social Science</span>
<span class="about-pill">AI Governance</span>
<span class="about-pill">Information Integrity</span>
<span class="about-pill">Digital Journalism</span>
<span class="about-pill">Platform Governance</span>
<span class="about-pill">Global South Technology Studies</span>
<span class="about-pill">Computational Media Analysis</span>
</div>

:::

::: {.about-card}

### Methods

<div class="about-pill-grid">
<span class="about-pill">Natural Language Processing</span>
<span class="about-pill">Computational Text Analysis</span>
<span class="about-pill">Statistical Analysis</span>
<span class="about-pill">Network Analysis</span>
<span class="about-pill">In-depth Interviewing</span>
<span class="about-pill">Thematic Analysis</span>
<span class="about-pill">Critical Discourse Analysis</span>
<span class="about-pill">Media Framing Analysis</span>
<span class="about-pill">Comparative Research</span>
<span class="about-pill">Mixed-methods Design</span>
</div>

:::


::: {.about-card .about-card-accent}

### Connect

<div class="about-connect-buttons">

<a class="about-connect-button"
   href="mailto:amaka.onebunne@temple.edu">
  <i class="bi bi-envelope" aria-hidden="true"></i>
  <span>Email Me</span>
</a>

<a class="about-connect-button"
   href="research.qmd">
  <i class="bi bi-folder2-open" aria-hidden="true"></i>
  <span>View My Research</span>
</a>

<a class="about-connect-button"
   href="assets/documents/amaka-onebunne-cv.pdf"
   target="_blank">
  <i class="bi bi-download" aria-hidden="true"></i>
  <span>Download CV</span>
</a>

</div>

:::
